description_long: This guard stone is a solid, elongated block with a rounded front that features a stylized, sculpted face, possibly representing an animal or mythological figure. The surface is rough and matte, with visible chisel marks and a weathered, stone-like texture. The head is slightly raised and detailed, while the body tapers smoothly toward the back, ending in a blunt, squared-off form. The object is monochromatic, predominantly off-white or light gray, suggesting it is carved from a single piece of stone. There are no visible openings, moving parts, or receptacle features, and the mass and solidity imply it is designed to be a fixed barrier. This type of guard stone would typically be found at the edge of a sidewalk, driveway, or building entrance to prevent vehicle encroachment and protect structures.
description: A solid, off-white stone guard stone with a sculpted face at one end and a tapered, blunt body. The surface is rough and weathered, with no visible openings or moving parts.
description_view_0: The front-left view highlights the sculpted face and rounded head, with the body tapering away from the viewer.
description_view_1: The left-side view shows the full profile of the face and the elongated, blocky body extending backward.
description_view_2: The rear-right view emphasizes the blunt, squared-off end and the smooth taper of the body, with minimal facial detail visible.
description_view_3: The right-side view displays the side of the head and the length of the body, with the facial features partially visible.
attribute_location: Typically placed at the entrance to driveways, along the edge of sidewalks, or near building corners in urban environments to prevent vehicle intrusion and protect structures.
attribute_installation_orientation: Meant to be fully above ground, with the base flush against the pavement or ground surface, and sometimes partially embedded for stability.
attribute_wear_patterns: Likely to show chipping and smoothing on the front and sides due to vehicle contact, as well as general surface erosion and discoloration from weather exposure. The top and facial features may be more smoothed from incidental contact and rainwater runoff.
